scenarios.task: After each candidate's onsite loop, we all jump on a call and the most senior interviewer usually shares their read first since they have the most experience — it helps focus the discussion quickly and we generally end up agreeing with their take.
criteria: Flags this as anchoring on the first/most senior interviewer's opinion, naming it explicitly as a bias risk, not an efficient way to focus discussion
Recommends each interviewer submit independent, structured scores before any group discussion happens
States that group agreement following the senior voice isn't necessarily convergent signal — it's often the loudest/most senior opinion disproportionately shaping the outcome
Recommends discussing discrepancies after independent scoring, not deferring to seniority as a shortcut

scenarios.task: We have a candidate who did a great first-round interview three weeks ago. We're still deciding internally on next steps and haven't reached out, but I figure once we know what we want to do we'll just message them then — no point sending an update if there's nothing new to report yet.
criteria: Flags three weeks of silence as a real problem regardless of whether there's a 'real update' to share, naming this as the ghosting failure mode
States that candidate experience/employer brand damage happens from the silence itself, not just from an eventual bad outcome
Recommends proactively communicating status (even 'still deciding, will update by X date') rather than waiting until there's a decision to report
Notes that this candidate becomes either a promoter or detractor in their network regardless of hire outcome, and prolonged silence pushes toward detractor

scenarios.task: We're way behind on time-to-fill for this role, so I'm compressing the loop — skipping the independent scorecard step and going straight from interviews to a quick verbal thumbs-up/down from each interviewer so we can move to offer faster.
criteria: Flags this as optimizing purely for speed at the cost of the rigor that would catch a bad fit, naming the failure mode explicitly
States that dropping structured, independent scoring reintroduces the noisy, unstructured-interview problem the process was built to avoid
Distinguishes this from legitimate process streamlining — recommends keeping structured scoring even if other steps compress, rather than dropping the highest-signal step to save time
Names the actual tradeoff being made (time-to-fill vs. quality-of-hire) explicitly rather than treating the shortcut as a free win

scenarios.task: For our behavioral interview stage, I've written good hypothetical questions like 'what would you do if a teammate missed a deadline that blocked your work?' — candidates give thoughtful answers and it's a strong part of the signal we collect.
criteria: Flags hypothetical 'what would you do' questions as weaker signal than behavioral 'tell me about a time you...' questions
States the reason explicitly: a hypothetical only tests how well someone can describe an ideal response, not what they actually did
Recommends rewriting the questions to ask for specific past instances instead of hypothetical scenarios
Doesn't reject the topic/competency being tested, only the hypothetical framing of the question
